subtopic_detail: Functional chronic constipation Movicol disimpaction and maintenance
stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 4-year-old non-Aboriginal girl with a 14-month history of infrequent painful bowel motions, with stool frequency of 1 to 2 per week, frequent staining of underwear with small amounts of liquid stool between hard motions, and observed stool-withholding behaviour. There is a 6-month history of intermittent abdominal pain. She has met all developmental milestones, is fully toilet trained for urine, and has no symptoms of urinary tract infection. Examination shows a palpable suprapubic mass consistent with faecal loading, normal anal position and tone, no sacral dimple, and normal lower limb neurology.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 °C, HR 104, BP not routinely measured at age 4, weight 17 kg (50th centile)
data_table.Examination: Palpable suprapubic faecal mass, normal anal exam, no anal fissures, normal perineum and sacrum, normal lower limb neurology
data_table.Investigations: No bloods or imaging required for diagnosis of functional constipation

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Increase dietary fibre to 18 grams daily through fruit, vegetables and wholegrains, encourage 1.5 litres of fluid intake per day, and reassess at 4 weeks before considering any pharmacological intervention or specific oral stool-softening therapy
options.rationale: Dietary advice alone in the presence of demonstrable faecal impaction is insufficient; disimpaction with osmotic laxatives is recommended first to break the cycle. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Refer urgently to paediatric gastroenterology for anorectal manometry and rectal biopsy to exclude Hirschsprung's disease given the duration of symptoms and presence of overflow soiling at the age of presentation
options.rationale: Hirschsprung's disease typically presents in infancy with delayed meconium passage; functional constipation does not require biopsy unless red flags are present. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Administer paraffin oil 5 mL orally twice daily and arrange daily glycerine suppositories for 7 days followed by ongoing rectal interventions if dietary modifications fail to soften stool after this initial disimpaction phase of intensive treatment
options.rationale: Oral disimpaction with high-dose polyethylene glycol is preferred over invasive rectal interventions in children, which can be traumatic and worsen stool-withholding behaviour. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Disimpact with high-dose polyethylene glycol 3350 with electrolytes (Movicol Junior) 4 sachets daily, increasing to 8 sachets daily as needed for 3 to 7 days until impaction clears, then transition to maintenance Movicol 1 to 2 sachets daily with toileting routines and parent education
options.rationale: RCH guidance recommends oral disimpaction with high-dose Movicol Junior (escalating regimen until impaction clears) followed by maintenance therapy with sustained Movicol for 3+ months alongside behavioural toileting program. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Prescribe lactulose 5 mL twice daily for 3 months as the only intervention required for functional childhood constipation alongside oral fluid encouragement and gradual increase in dietary fibre over the next month
options.rationale: Disimpaction must precede maintenance; lactulose alone without disimpaction or behavioural intervention in the setting of faecal impaction is inadequate. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

explanation.summary: Functional constipation in children is the cause of over 90% of presentations. Diagnosis is clinical (Rome IV criteria: 2+ of 2 or fewer defecations per week, history of retentive posturing, history of painful or hard motions, large diameter stools, faecal mass on exam, soiling in toilet-trained child). Red flags suggesting organic cause include delayed meconium past 48 hours, failure to thrive, fever, vomiting, abnormal anal exam or neurology, sacral abnormalities. Management is disimpaction with oral PEG (Movicol Junior, escalating doses), then maintenance for at least 3 months, plus toileting behavioural program. Avoid rectal interventions where possible.
key_points: Functional constipation: clinical diagnosis (Rome IV), 90% of cases
Red flags: delayed meconium, FTT, abnormal anal exam, neurology
Disimpaction: Movicol Junior 4-8 sachets daily over 3-7 days
Maintenance: Movicol 1-2 sachets daily for at least 3 months
Behavioural: regular toileting after meals, reward charts
explanation.pearls: Premature cessation of maintenance laxatives in the first 6 months is the single most common cause of relapse; counsel families that hard-stool memories take months to fade and that staying on maintenance through a painless interval is the actual treatment, not just a dose.

sources.label: NICE CG99: Constipation in children and young people

subtopic_detail: Henoch-Schonlein purpura with renal involvement and timing of follow-up
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 7-year-old boy in the emergency department with a 5-day history of a palpable purpuric rash over the buttocks and lower limbs, intermittent colicky abdominal pain for 3 days, and now a swollen painful right ankle. He has been previously well, fully immunised, no recent medication, and had a viral upper respiratory tract infection 2 weeks ago. He is haemodynamically stable. On examination there is a typical non-blanching palpable purpuric rash on the lower limbs and buttocks, a swollen tender right ankle, mild abdominal tenderness without peritonism, and normal genitalia.

data_table.Examination: Palpable purpura buttocks/lower limbs, right ankle swollen tender, abdomen mildly tender, no peritonism, normal scrotum
data_table.Investigations: FBC: WBC 9.4, Hb 124, plt 320; UEC normal; urinalysis: blood 2+, protein 1+, no casts; urinary albumin-creatinine ratio 18 mg/mmol; BP 102/64

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan?

options.text: Admit for IV methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg daily for 3 days then oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 6 weeks and arrange urgent renal biopsy because of the proteinuria and microscopic haematuria at presentation
options.rationale: ACR 18 mg/mmol with blood 2+ is not nephrotic-range and does not warrant biopsy or pulsed steroids; HSP nephritis is monitored with urine and BP at follow-up. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura (IgA vasculitis))

options.text: Discharge home with simple analgesia, supportive care, and a structured follow-up schedule of weekly urinalysis and blood pressure for 2 months and then 3-monthly checks for 12 months to detect delayed HSP nephritis
options.rationale: Typical HSP with mild renal findings is managed with home follow-up; serial urinalysis and BP are recommended weekly for 1-2 months then 3-6 monthly for at least 12 months to detect delayed nephritis. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura (IgA vasculitis))

options.text: Admit for observation with intravenous cefotaxime 50 mg/kg every 8 hours empirically until a complete septic workup including blood cultures, CSF and lumbar puncture confirms or excludes meningococcal sepsis
options.rationale: Palpable purpura in HSP is a vasculitic eruption not meningococcal sepsis; the child is well, afebrile with characteristic distribution. Septic workup and broad antibiotics are not indicated. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura (IgA vasculitis))

options.text: Admit for monitoring and arrange abdominal ultrasound urgently to exclude intussusception given the abdominal pain, before reassessment to determine outpatient management at the end of the admission period
options.rationale: HSP can cause intussusception (typically ileoileal) but it is not the most likely cause of mild abdominal pain in this child; routine US is not indicated unless pain is severe, persistent or there is bilious vomiting or PR bleeding. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura (IgA vasculitis))

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 14 days for arthralgia and abdominal pain without altering renal follow-up plan, on the basis that early steroids prevent progression to chronic HSP nephritis
options.rationale: Steroids do not prevent or alter incidence of HSP nephritis; they are reserved for severe symptoms (significant abdominal pain, scrotal involvement, severe arthralgia, established nephritis with crescents). (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura (IgA vasculitis))

explanation.summary: Henoch-Schonlein purpura (IgA vasculitis) is the commonest small-vessel vasculitis of childhood. Diagnosis is clinical: palpable purpura with at least one of arthritis/arthralgia, abdominal pain, biopsy-proven IgA deposition or renal involvement. Renal involvement (microscopic haematuria, proteinuria, hypertension, nephritis) occurs in 20 to 40% and is the major long-term morbidity. Most cases self-resolve. Monitoring: urinalysis and BP weekly for 1-2 months, then 3-monthly for 12 months. Refer to nephrology if nephrotic-range proteinuria (UPCR over 200 mg/mmol or ACR over 250 mg/mmol), persistent significant proteinuria, hypertension, eGFR decline or significant haematuria/casts. Steroids do not prevent nephritis but are used for severe symptoms.
key_points: HSP: palpable purpura + arthritis/abd pain/IgA biopsy/renal involvement
Renal involvement in 20-40%; major long-term morbidity
Monitoring: urinalysis + BP weekly for 1-2 months then 3-monthly for 12 months
Steroids: severe abdominal pain, scrotal involvement, established nephritis
Refer nephrology: nephrotic-range, persistent significant proteinuria, hypertension, eGFR decline
explanation.pearls: Scrotal involvement in HSP can mimic testicular torsion; bedside ultrasound showing scrotal wall oedema with normal testicular Doppler flow supports HSP scrotal involvement and avoids unnecessary surgical exploration.

sources.label: Royal College of Paediatrics: HSP guideline

subtopic_detail: Neonatal jaundice phototherapy threshold and risk-factor adjustment
stem: You are the paediatric resident in the postnatal ward reviewing a 3-day-old term female neonate (40+2 weeks at birth, birthweight 3350 g) brought to the special care nursery because of yellowing of the skin extending to the trunk. She is breastfeeding 8 times per day, has wet nappies and is alert and active. Born by uncomplicated SVD. Mother's blood group A positive, infant's blood group B positive, direct Coombs negative. Total serum bilirubin taken at 72 hours of age is 290 micromol/L (unconjugated 282), conjugated 8. The mother is Rh negative and received antenatal anti-D appropriately.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 142, RR 44, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Visible jaundice cephalocaudally to the chest, alert and active, normal tone, no cephalohaematoma or bruising, normal cardiac and respiratory exam
data_table.Investigations: TSB 290 micromol/L at 72 hours, conjugated 8, unconjugated 282, Hb 168 g/L, reticulocytes 4%, Coombs negative, blood group B+, maternal A+

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure mother that the bilirubin level is below the phototherapy threshold for a term well infant at 72 hours, advise on continued breastfeeding 8 to 12 times a day and arrange repeat bilirubin level at 96 hours
options.rationale: TSB 290 at 72 hours in a term infant crosses the phototherapy threshold per AAP/RCH nomograms; deferral risks further rise toward exchange-transfusion territory. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Arrange urgent exchange transfusion because the bilirubin level of 290 micromol/L is in the kernicterus risk zone in a 72-hour-old infant and immediate dual-volume exchange transfusion is mandated to prevent neurological injury
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion threshold in a term infant at 72 hours is around 425 micromol/L (AAP) with no risk factors; 290 is below exchange threshold and warrants phototherapy first. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Investigate further with thyroid function, urine for reducing substances and G6PD screen given the elevated bilirubin and risk of metabolic causes before starting phototherapy because the level is unusual for breastfeeding-related jaundice
options.rationale: Breastfeeding-related jaundice and ABO setoff are reasonable considerations; investigations are useful but should not delay initiation of phototherapy at this level. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 1 g/kg over 4 hours because of the presence of ABO blood group setoff between mother (A) and infant (B) and the rising bilirubin trajectory, before considering phototherapy
options.rationale: IVIG is used for isoimmune haemolytic disease (Rh or ABO) with positive Coombs and rising bilirubin despite phototherapy approaching exchange threshold; Coombs is negative here. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Commence intensive (multi-light) phototherapy with monitoring of bilirubin in 6 hours, support continued breastfeeding with potential top-ups if hydration is suboptimal, and review for risk factors that would lower the threshold
options.rationale: TSB 290 at 72 hours crosses the phototherapy threshold for a term infant without risk factors; intensive phototherapy, ongoing feeding support and repeat TSB in 6 hours are recommended. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

explanation.summary: Neonatal jaundice is common; in term well infants without haemolysis the AAP/RCH 2022 nomogram thresholds for phototherapy at 72 hours are approximately 290 to 300 micromol/L (with thresholds adjusted lower with risk factors: gestational age under 38 weeks, isoimmune haemolytic disease, G6PD deficiency, sepsis, significant lethargy or temperature instability). Exchange transfusion thresholds at 72 hours are around 425 micromol/L for term infants without risk factors. Phototherapy is given with intensive blue spectrum, feeding maintained, repeat TSB in 4 to 6 hours. Investigate prolonged jaundice (over 14 days term, 21 days preterm) for cholestasis, infection, hypothyroidism, metabolic causes.
key_points: Term well 72h: phototherapy at TSB approx 290-300 micromol/L
Adjust lower with risk factors: gestation under 38wk, haemolysis, G6PD, sepsis
Exchange transfusion term 72h: TSB approx 425 micromol/L
IVIG: Coombs-positive haemolysis approaching exchange
Investigate prolonged jaundice over 14 days term
explanation.pearls: Visual estimation of jaundice severity is unreliable past the umbilicus; always confirm with TSB or transcutaneous bilirubinometer (TcB) and plot on age-in-hours nomogram - the trajectory matters more than the static number.

sources.label: AAP Clinical Practice Guideline: Hyperbilirubinemia in Newborn Infant 2022

subtopic_detail: Preschool viral-induced wheeze first-line bronchodilator and steroid evidence
stem: You are the emergency department paediatric registrar reviewing an 18-month-old boy with the third episode in 6 months of wheeze, cough and increased work of breathing during a coryzal illness. He responded to salbutamol via spacer at home this morning but has worsened over the day with persistent wheeze and tachypnoea. His first wheeze episode was at 8 months. There is no atopic history (no eczema, no food allergy), no parental asthma, and no inhaled smoke exposure. He has met all developmental milestones and growth is on the 50th centile.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4 °C, HR 152, RR 48, SpO2 92% room air, BP not routine
data_table.Examination: Moderate work of breathing with subcostal recession, audible expiratory wheeze, prolonged expiratory phase, air entry symmetrical with widespread polyphonic wheeze, alert, mildly distressed
data_table.Investigations: No bloods or chest X-ray indicated routinely; NPS not required

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in his management?

options.text: Administer back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg every 20 minutes for 3 doses with high-flow oxygen, add nebulised ipratropium bromide 250 micrograms every 20 minutes and arrange admission for IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg every 6 hours
options.rationale: Salbutamol via spacer (6 puffs at this size) is preferred over nebuliser; oral prednisolone is preferred over IV in moderate exacerbation tolerating oral; routine ipratropium is reserved for severe disease. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia))
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Discharge home with an oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 3 days, salbutamol via spacer 6 puffs every 4 hours as required, with safety-net advice and review at 24 hours by GP for ongoing management of the acute episode
options.rationale: SpO2 92% with moderate work of breathing requires in-department observation with bronchodilator and oxygen titration before considering discharge; not an immediate discharge candidate. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia))
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Administer salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer every 20 minutes for 3 doses with oxygen to maintain SpO2 above 94%, give oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg as a single dose, and reassess after the third salbutamol round before deciding admission
options.rationale: Moderate preschool wheeze responds to salbutamol via spacer (6 puffs) every 20 minutes for 3 doses; oral prednisolone in modest episodes in children with a clear viral-induced wheeze pattern who present to ED is now supported in Australian guidance. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia))
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Commence inhaled fluticasone propionate 100 micrograms twice daily as a preventer from today given the recurrent wheeze episodes and review symptom control in 4 weeks before adjusting therapy or stepping up to combination inhaler
options.rationale: ICS preventer in pure viral-induced preschool wheeze without interval symptoms or atopy has limited benefit; initiation should be considered after 3+ episodes when guided by phenotype, and not as the acute ED intervention. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia))
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Administer nebulised adrenaline 1:1000 5 mL with oxygen as is given for moderate-severe croup, on the basis that the wheeze could reflect upper airway involvement that has been mislabelled as lower airway disease
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is for stridor in croup, not for lower-airway wheeze; this is not appropriate for viral-induced wheeze. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia))
source_refs: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

explanation.summary: Preschool wheeze (under 5 years) is heterogeneous - episodic viral-induced wheeze (no interval symptoms, no atopy) versus multi-trigger wheeze (likely asthma trajectory, often atopic). Acute moderate exacerbation: salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer (12 puffs for over 6 years) every 20 minutes for 3 doses with oxygen titrated to keep SpO2 over 94%. Oral prednisolone (1-2 mg/kg up to 50 mg) for moderate/severe exacerbations is recommended in Australian guidance even for preschool age presenting to ED. Ipratropium adds benefit in severe/life-threatening. ICS preventer use in pure viral-induced wheeze is debated; consider after 3+ episodes in a year, family history of asthma, or interval symptoms. Spacer technique is critical.
key_points: Acute moderate preschool wheeze: salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer q20min x3
Oral prednisolone 1-2 mg/kg (max 50 mg) for moderate/severe exacerbation
Ipratropium: add for severe/life-threatening
Spacer with mask under 4 years, mouthpiece from 4-5 years
ICS preventer: consider after 3+ episodes, atopy or interval symptoms
explanation.pearls: An infant under 12 months with a first wheeze illness is bronchiolitis not asthma until proven otherwise; reaching for salbutamol there is not evidence-based, but in a toddler with recurrent wheeze responding to bronchodilator, the phenotype tips toward asthma even before formal diagnosis.

sources.label: Australian Asthma Handbook (National Asthma Council Australia)

subtopic_detail: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension recognition and lumbar puncture opening pressure
stem: You are the medical registrar in the assessment unit reviewing a 26-year-old woman with 6 weeks of progressively worsening daily headache, retro-orbital pressure, pulsatile tinnitus and transient visual obscurations on standing. She has gained 8 kg over 6 months and her BMI is now 32. She is on no regular medication. She has no history of head trauma, neoplasia or immunosuppression. Fundoscopy shows bilateral papilloedema with peripapillary haemorrhages. MRI brain with venography is normal with no mass, hydrocephalus or sinus thrombosis. Cranial nerve examination shows a bilateral abducens nerve weakness with normal visual acuity.

data_table.Examination: Bilateral papilloedema with peripapillary haemorrhages, bilateral CN VI weakness, otherwise normal cranial nerves and limb examination
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain with venography normal; bloods FBC, UEC, LFTs, B12, TSH normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Lumbar puncture in the lateral decubitus position with measurement of opening pressure and CSF studies including cell count, glucose and protein to confirm idiopathic intracranial hypertension before initiating treatment
options.rationale: After secondary causes are excluded by neuroimaging (including venography), LP with opening pressure over 250 mmCSF (over 280 in BMI over 30) and normal CSF composition confirms IIH (modified Dandy criteria). (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension

options.text: Initiate acetazolamide 250 mg twice daily empirically and arrange follow-up in 2 weeks for review of papilloedema and visual fields without further diagnostic investigation given the typical clinical pattern of IIH
options.rationale: Treatment should follow diagnostic confirmation by LP (Dandy criteria); empirical treatment before confirmation risks missing alternative causes such as hidden venous sinus pathology. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension

options.text: Arrange CT venogram or MR venogram to exclude cerebral venous sinus thrombosis as the primary cause of raised intracranial pressure given the clinical features and presence of papilloedema and CN VI palsy
options.rationale: She has already had MRI brain WITH venography - this has been done. The next step is LP with opening pressure. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension

options.text: Refer urgently to neurosurgery for consideration of optic nerve sheath fenestration and ventriculoperitoneal shunting given the bilateral papilloedema and risk of permanent visual loss without immediate decompression
options.rationale: Surgical interventions are reserved for fulminant IIH or progressive visual loss despite medical therapy; diagnostic workup including LP is required first. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension

options.text: Send a lumbar CSF for autoimmune encephalitis and paraneoplastic antibody panels and arrange whole-body PET-CT to look for occult malignancy given the subacute course and weight gain over the last 6 months
options.rationale: Routine paraneoplastic and autoimmune workup is not part of initial IIH evaluation; the clinical and imaging features are typical for IIH and do not suggest paraneoplastic. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension)
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension

explanation.summary: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension typically affects obese women of reproductive age. Modified Dandy criteria: symptoms/signs of raised ICP (headache, papilloedema, pulsatile tinnitus, transient visual obscurations, CN VI palsy), no localising features beyond CN VI, normal CSF composition, opening pressure over 250 mmCSF (over 280 if BMI over 30), no other identifiable cause on neuroimaging including venography. Treatment: weight loss (5 to 10%) is the most effective long-term measure. Acetazolamide first-line pharmacotherapy, topiramate second-line. Surgical options for fulminant or progressive visual loss: optic nerve sheath fenestration, lumboperitoneal or ventriculoperitoneal shunt, or venous sinus stenting where stenosis is documented.
key_points: IIH demographic: obese women of reproductive age
Modified Dandy: symptoms ICP + papilloedema + normal CSF + OP over 250 (280 if BMI over 30)
Imaging must include venography to exclude CVST
First-line: weight loss + acetazolamide; second-line topiramate
Visual loss: ONSF, shunt, or sinus stenting (if stenosis)
explanation.pearls: Visual loss in IIH occurs through papilloedema-induced optic atrophy, not directly from raised ICP; ophthalmology co-management with formal visual fields every 1-3 months in untreated/severe cases drives treatment escalation before permanent loss.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension
RANZCO: Papilloedema and raised intracranial pressure
